channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Warhammer 40K Ciaphas Cain Review - For The Emperor
item.content:encoded:
Another hero of the Imperium returns from their reported death. Commissar Ciaphas Cain leaps from the pages of the Black Library novels onto the tabletop with a brand-new miniature and 11th Edition 40K rules, alongside some individual Imperial Guard releases. In this article, we check out Cain's miniature and rules, and those of the Hippogriff AFV and Centaur RSV.

We've been covering Warhammer 40K for a while on the site, and you can check out our coverage of the recently released 40K 11th Edition launch box, and guide to getting started with Warhammer 40K with Combat Patrol.

Commissar Cain isn't your usual Commissar. Rather than be like the combat-monster Yarrick, Cain actively tries not to get himself killed and usually ends up leading elements of the Imperium to incredible victories. Cain has been brought to life through a series of Black Library novels, first published in 2003, and has finally got a miniature and rules for 40K. 

Cain's datasheet does come under the Warhammer Legends rules, which means that he can't be used for tournament play, but can be used for friendly or narrative gaming. This is probably because Cain has had a full state funeral after his death, but because he has cheated death more than once, he has a standing rule that he's always considered on active service, just in case he appears in the middle of the action once again.

Cain's miniature comes with his sidekick Jurgen, and they can either be deployed together on a scenic base, or separately on individual bases, where Cain's miniature is used for all rules purposes, and Jurgen's acts a token.

On the tabletop, reflecting Cain's ability to always be in the thick of the fighting, no matter how hard he tries to avoid it, his Trapped by Excuses rule means that he can't be deployed in reserves and must always be deployed on the battlefield. Cain is also pretty survivable, with a standard 4+ save, or a 5+ invulnerable save, along with an additional 4+ save against psychic attacks and mortal wounds. He can also use his once-per-game ability to give him a 2+ invulnerable save for a phase.

Cain comes armed with precision laspistols (to target characters within units) and a chainsword, and Jurgen backs him up with a Meltagun to tackle any armored units that get too close. Cain has several options for units to attach to, and allows any friendly Astra Militarum unit within 6 inches to reroll battleshock tests, and can issue 2 orders to regiment units a turn, limited to Duty and Honor, Move Move Move and Take Cover.

Hippogriff AFV

We first saw the Hippogriff AFV in the Armageddon launch wave, as part of the Astra Militarum Armageddon Battalion, and now this light tank gets its individual release. 

The Hippogriff AFV has a choice of 4 main weapons:

  • The Vigilator Cannon with a range of 36, and D6 strength 8, 2 damage attacks
  • The Chiron Gatling Cannon with range 24, and 12 strength 5, 1 damage attacks
  • The Meltacannon, with range 18, and 2 strength 9, -4 AP D6 damage attacks
  • The Lascannon, with range 48, and 1 strength 12, -3 AP and D6+1 damage attacks. 

It also has a choice of secondary weapon between the longer range heavy stubber, or shorter range but higher strength and AP meltagun. Once it's fired each turn, the Hippogriff AFV can also make a bonus D6 move to nip it into cover if required.

The choice of weapons and extra mobility make this a great light tank to back up infantry or heavier tanks, or just use as a forward assault piece to tie up the enemy while other forces position or move forward.

Centaur RSV

Like the Hippogriff, the Centaur RSV also first appeared in the Armageddon launch wave as part of the Astra Militarum Armageddon Battalion, and now also gets its own individual release. The Centaur RSV is an open-air troop transport that, as well as having its own pintle-mounted heavy stubber, allows up to 12 troops mounted within to add their firepower from the firing deck. 

It has a move of 12, and an objective control of 2, which is boosted by 1 for every 3 infantry it carries, so you can have a fast-moving objective control 6 vehicle that everyone can still fire from. You could throw 10 Cadian Shock Troops in, which you can upgrade 2 of to carry more powerful weapons with which to fire from the Centaur RSV, or you could add in some Kasrkin who carry up to 4 special weapons, or just load it up with Ogryn's (who each count as 3 troopers for load capacity) for some Ripper Gun fun.

Warhammer 40K Ciaphas Cain Final Thoughts?

While Ciaphas Cain isn't a beast on the tabletop, and only has Legends rules, it's still great to finally get the option to use him in games, and the miniature looks great. His rules capture the spirit of the character well, and his ability to always get into trouble, no matter how hard he tries, and survive it against the odds. I've been a fan of the 2 new vehicles since their release with the Armageddon wave. The Hippogriff AFV is a great light tank, extremely mobile, with a great choice of weapons, and the Centaur RSV is a fun little transport that gives very Orky vibes with loads of troops squashed in the back, trying to bring their weapons to bear.

The copies of Warhammer 40K Ciaphas Cain, Hippogriff AFV and Centaur RSV used to produce this review were provided by Games Workshop.

Adam is a Tabletop Specialist for TechRaptor. He started writing for TechRaptor in 2017 and took over as Tabletop Editor in 2019 and has since stood down… More about Adam



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/reviews/warhammer-40k-ciaphas-cain-review-for-emperor
